quality.rationale: 将随机行走推广至曲线速度空间，结合相对论快度与扩散方程推导渐近分布，逻辑严密且具清晰物理图像

origin.question: Problem 7.22. An inebriated astronaut pulses his rocket, firing in a random direction for each pulse. As measured in a momentarily comoving frame, each pulse corresponds to a velocity boost of $\Delta v << c$ . Find the probability distribution of his resultant velocity after $n$ boosts, where $n$ is a very large number. Show that the drunk astronaut achieves highly relativistic velocities less efficiently than a sober astronaut (who fires his rocket always in the same direction), and takes on the average $3c / \Delta v$ times as many pulses to achieve the same velocity.
origin.answer: Velocity-space probability distribution: $P(\\underline{v}, n) = (4 \\pi)^{-3/2} e^{-t} t^{-3/2} \\frac{\\Psi}{\\sin h \\Psi} e^{-(\\Psi^2 / 4t)}$ with $t \\equiv n\\Delta v^2 / 6$. Mean rapidity grows as $\\langle \\Psi \\rangle \\approx 2t$. The drunk astronaut requires $3c/\\Delta v$ times as many pulses as a sober astronaut to reach the same velocity.
origin.solution:
Solution 7.22. For clarity first consider the Newtonian case. Let $\mathbf{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\underline{\mathbf{n}})$ be the probability of the velocity being $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ after $\mathbf{n}$ boosts. We want to find a differential equation for $\mathbf{P}$ . To accomplish this note that if the velocity is $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ at step $\mathbf{n}$ then at step $\mathbf{n} - 1$ the velocity must have been somewhere in velocity space at a distance $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ from $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ . By symmetry $\mathbf{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\underline{\mathbf{n}})$ has equal contributions from points on the sphere of radius
$\Delta \mathbf{v}$ about $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ so we can equate $\mathbb{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\mathbf{n})$ with the isotropic average of $\mathbb{P}$ , at $\mathbf{n} - 1$ , over that sphere:
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}}, n) = < \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}} + \Delta v, n - 1) >_{\text {s p h e r e}} \\ = (1 / 6) \left[ P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}}_{x}, n - 1\right) + P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}}_{y}, n - 1\right) + P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}}_{z}, n - 1\right) \right. \\ + \mathrm {P} (\underline {\mathrm {v}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}}_{\mathrm {x}}, n - 1) + \mathrm {P} (\underline {\mathrm {v}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}}_{\mathrm {y}}, n - 1) + \mathrm {P} (\underline {\mathrm {v}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}}_{z}, n - 1) ] \\ \approx \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}}, n - 1) + (\Delta v)^{2} \nabla^{2} \mathrm {P} / 6. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$
The differential equation then, is
$$
\frac {\partial \mathbf {P}}{\partial \mathrm {n}} = \frac {(\Delta \mathbf {v})^{2}}{6} \nabla^{2} \mathbf {P}, \tag {2}
$$
the standard diffusion equation. Its solution, with $\mathrm{P}(\underline{\mathrm{v}},0) = \delta^3 (\underline{\mathrm{v}})$ is
$$
P (v, n) = (4 \pi)^{- \frac {3}{2}} (n \Delta v^{2} / 6)^{- \frac {3}{2}} \exp \left(\frac {- 3 v^{2}}{2 n \Delta v^{2}}\right).
$$
In the relativistic problem the same arguments hold except that velocities do not add linearly; we must use the relativistic velocity addition formulas. Since $\Delta v \ll c$ , however, the proper velocity $\Delta v$ does add linearly in a frame momentarily comoving with the rocket ship. Thus, the diffusion Equation (2) is locally valid in velocity space; we only need take into account that the global metric of relativistic velocity space is different. This is done by solving Equation (2) with $
abla^2$ representing the Laplacian in a curved-metric velocity space, with metric (see Problem 6.8).
$$
\mathrm {d v}_{\text {p r o p e r}}^{2} = \mathrm {d} \Psi^{2} + \sin h^{2} \Psi \left(\mathrm {d v}_{\theta}^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d v}_{\phi}^{2}\right) \tag {4}
$$
where $\Psi$ is the rapidity parameter, that is $	an h \Psi \equiv |\underline{\mathbf{v}}|$ . Using the relation [Problem 7.7 (j)]
$$
P_{; a}^{; a} = g^{- \frac {1}{2}} \left(g^{\frac {1}{2}} g^{\alpha \beta} P, \beta\right), a \tag {5}
$$
and the fact that by spherical symmetry $ rac{\partial\mathbf{P}}{\partial\mathbf{v}_{\phi}} = \frac{\partial\mathbf{P}}{\partial\mathbf{v}_{\theta}} = 0$ we get
$$
\frac {\partial \mathrm {P}}{\partial \mathrm {n}} = \frac {\Delta \mathrm {v}^{2}}{6} \left[ \frac {1}{\sin h^{2} \Psi} \frac {\partial}{\partial \Psi} \left(\sin h^{2} \Psi \frac {\partial \mathrm {P}}{\partial \Psi}\right) \right]. \tag {6}
$$
It is not difficult to check that this has the solution
$$
\mathrm {P} (\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {v}}, \mathrm {n}) = (4 \pi)^{- \frac {3}{2}} \mathrm {e}^{- \mathrm {t}} \mathrm {t}^{- \frac {3}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{\sin h \Psi} \mathrm {e}^{- \left(\Psi^{2} / 4 \mathrm {t}\right)}, \tag {7}
$$
where $t \equiv n\Delta v^2 / 6$ . This agrees with the Newtonian solution Equation (3) when $t \ll 1$ , $\Psi \ll 1$ , (no restriction on $\Psi^2 / 4t$ ).
The probability of $\Psi$ between $\Psi$ and $\Psi + \mathrm{d}\Psi$ is (using the velocity-space metric) $4\pi \sin h^2\Psi \mathrm{Pd}\Psi$ which for $t >> 1$ , $\Psi >> 1$ has the asymptotic behavior
$$
\frac {d P}{d \Psi} \approx (4 \pi)^{- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{t^{\frac {3}{2}}} e^{- \Psi^{2} / 4 t - t + \Psi} = (4 \pi)^{- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{t^{\frac {3}{2}}} e^{- t (1 - \frac {\Psi}{2 t})^{2}}.
$$
Only the exponential is of significance here, and this tells us that $\Psi$ has a mean value $<\Psi> \approx 2t$ , and a standard deviation $\Delta\Psi \sim \sqrt{2t}$ , so $\Delta\Psi/<\Psi> \to 0$ as $t \to \inf ty$ . The mean rapidity $<\Psi>$ increases linearly with number of steps, but the average boost per step is $\Delta v^2/3$ . A sober pilot would increase his rapidity by $\Delta v$ each step, $3c/\Delta v$ times as much. Nevertheless it is amazing that the drunk astronaut does as well as he does; in the Newtonian case velocity or rapidity increased only as $n^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , not as $n$ . The reason here has to do with the Lorentz transformation: if an observer moving away from you chooses a random direction to fire a bullet, the directions look biased outward from you (headlight effect).
